title: Chapter 21: Meeting at the Edge of the Abyss
paragraphs: The Gates of Lamentation lived up to their name. On the southeastern edge of Agdash's outer ring, the architecture surrendered under the weight of a rotting reality. Ancient runes here had long since faded, and the marble was shot through with ugly, black cracks from which oozed an acrid slime smelling of ozone and decay. The magical barrier over this sector had thinned to the state of a soap bubble—through it, crimson, pulsing scars in the fabric of the universe could be seen with terrifying clarity. The ash fell thicker here, covering the dead earth with a solid gray carpet.
It was here that Lord Valtor had sent the four outcasts. To a certain, quiet death.
When Yashil, Kirah, Janavar, and Renée climbed onto the semi-ruined gatehouse tower, they expected to see emptiness. But the sector was not abandoned.

At the edge of a broken parapet, with his back to them, stood a figure, motionless. Heavy armor, covered in centuries-old dents, seemed woven from the night itself. Instead of hair above the elf's pale, pointed profile, an absolutely black flame writhed slowly and ominously. It did not radiate light—it greedily consumed it, drawing warmth from the surrounding air.

Slightly below, in the shelter of a semi-collapsed arch, lay a strange camp. A tall man with fair hair and an unexpectedly clear, keen gaze was wiping a curious crossbow without a bowstring with a rag. Beside him, a huge, broad-shouldered man was repeatedly clenching and unclenching a massive steel gauntlet with a clang, from which steam escaped with a hiss. A little further away, leaning against the wall, another guy slept serenely, his hands sparking with faint blue discharges even in his sleep. Two more were quietly arguing over a map spread out on a stone, moving some gears around.

Kirah froze, as if struck in the chest by an invisible spear. The huge demon backed away, his claws scratching furrows into the stone with a panic-stricken scrape. The scent emanating from the figure on the wall was familiar to every creature of Zakhan. It was the scent of their own, inevitable doom.
"Yashil..." Kirah exhaled, and his voice broke into a pitiful, vibrating rasp. "It's him. The Black Flame. The last disciple of Sivarus. We need to leave. Now."
But the sound of the demon's claws had already broken the dead silence.

The black flame on Avotu's head instantly, hungrily shot upward. The monster-slayer's instincts, honed by centuries of pain and loss, worked faster than thought. The elf turned smoothly. His hand in its plate gauntlet came to rest on the hilt of Nature's Blade. Metal met the dead air, and the sword emitted a low, vibrating hum, like the whisper of a nascent tornado.
Avotu's eyes—two bottomless wells of emptiness—unerrily picked out the huge silhouette of a high demon in the gray fog.

In the camp below, all movement instantly ceased. Alexander raised his mag-crossbow, Ravan stepped forward with a dull thud, raising his pneumo-fist, and Anar, opening one eye, lazily but lethally snapped his fingers, summoning a branching arc of lightning.

"Auf," Janavar said quietly, without his usual mockery, gripping his jagged axe with both paws. The fur on the werewolf's scruff stood on end. "It seems we've skipped levels and come out straight to the final boss."

Renée slowly removed the mouthpiece from her mouth. Her amber eyes narrowed. For the first time in a long time, the fox's show of indifference failed her. She felt the power emanating from the fire elf. This strength could wipe them into powder faster than the ash could fall.

Avotu took a step forward. The air around him began to whirl into a dense abrasive vortex. He saw before him only the ugly mask of the Legion. Another monster spawned by the universe's cancerous tumor.
But before Nature's Blade could describe a lethal arc, Yashil did what he always did.
The small goblin calmly stepped forward, standing exactly halfway between the blade of the great hero and the demon trembling with terror.

The fierce wind that broke from Avotu's blade struck Yashil, nearly knocking him off his feet. The goblin's old clothes fluttered, but he dug his bare feet into the stone crumbles and did not budge an inch.

"He will not raise a hand against you, Avotu of Agdash," Yashil's voice was not loud, but it carried that same ancient, crystal-clear truth that had recently broken the lieutenant at the gates. "And I will not allow you to raise a blade against him."

Avotu froze. The black flame on his head twitched in confusion. In all his incredibly long life, saturated with blood, no being had ever stood between him and his target without baring a weapon. And as for goblins, he had always considered them timid scavengers.
"Step aside, green-skin," the elf's voice was colder than ice. "Your companion is a Reaper. His nature is to kill."

"His nature is choice," Yashil replied, not backing down. He looked into the black voids of the elf's eyes, and Avotu suddenly felt someone looking into the very depths of his own scars. "I know how many like him you have committed to the earth. I can smell their ash on your armor. But this demon weeps over dead birds. Kill me first if you believe that in this rotting world there is no room left for mercy."

Avotu's hand trembled. Nature's Blade, ready to burst into a storm, rang out piteously and began to quiet down. The elf looked at the goblin, then shifted his gaze to the huge demon, who indeed was not attempting to attack. Kirah was on his knees, covering with his paws not himself, but preparing to shield Yashil in case of a blow. Behind stood the werewolf, ready to throw himself onto a certain death for the sake of the little green one, and the fox, whose eyes expressed not fear, but a bitter solidarity.
It was absurd. It was illogical.
And it was painfully familiar.

Heavy footsteps were heard from behind. Alexander, lowering his crossbow, approached Avotu and gently placed a palm on the elf's steel-clad shoulder. The man from another dimension carefully scanned the motley company of outcasts.

"Lower your sword, brother," Sasha said quietly, in an earthily simple way. A faint, understanding smile appeared on his lips. Seeing a goblin ready to die for a demon, he remembered how Anar had burned his nerves for Ravan's sake, and Avotu himself had sheltered them all from the storm. "It seems we're not the only stray dogs in this universe that have huddles into a pack."

Avotu slowly, with a dry click, returned Nature's Blade to its scabbard. The wind instantly subsided. The black flame on his head, which a second ago had been blazed with rage, suddenly settled, becoming soft, almost warm.

The great warrior, on whose shoulders lay the weight of guilt for an entire ruined world, looked at the small goblin, and in his dead, scorched soul, a fragile sprout was blossoming. He had spent his whole life searching for evil to punish it. And this goblin sought good where it by definition could not be—and found it.
"The High Elders sent you here to die," Avotu said. It was not a question, but a statement of fact.

"They called it an honorable exile," Renée spoke up, elegantly clicking her lighter and lighting her cigarette. She finally allowed herself to relax. "But we know how to read between the lines, pointy-ears."
"My name is Yashil," the goblin placed a hand to his chest. "We came to defend the Gates of Lamentation."
Ravan, who had come up behind Alexander, struck his pneumo-fist against the open palm of his left hand with a crash. The sound was impressive.
"Well, then you're in luck, boys," the former dentist grinned broadly. "Zhenya and I just reinforced the beams here. And Yura drew up a guard schedule. We'll be dying strictly by the regulations."

Avotu lowered himself onto the stone parapet, leaning his forearms heavily on his knees. He looked at his band of humans from another world, devoid of magic but full of steel will. And at Yashil's band—monsters whom the world had rejected, but who refused to reject the world.
For the first time in long decades, Avotu laughed. It was a quiet, cracked laugh of a being that had understood the greatest irony of the universe.

"The fabric of the universe is rotting," the fire elf said, looking at the ashen sky. "The gods, if they ever were, have long since abandoned us. But as long as a goblin forgives a demon, and humans without magic forge steel to protect an elf... the Void hasn't the slightest chance of breaking us from within."

Yashil came and sat beside Avotu. A small green figure and a demigod clad in black steel. they sat on the edge of a fading eternity and watched as through the gray ash broke the dim but stubborn light of sickly stars.
The Gates of Lamentation were no longer the weakest point of Agdash. On this night, they became the safest place in the entire doomed world.
